Level 1: Foundations (Men, Age 19–30)
Question 1: The Professional Handshake & Eye Contact
Scenario: You are being introduced to a new male project lead. How do you establish an immediate "Professional Mirror" of reliability?
A) ❌ Would not do that — Give a very firm, almost painful "bone-crusher" grip to show you are the alpha.
This is seen as "brash" and insecure. In the middle-class matrix, physical aggression — even in a handshake — is unrefined and breaks social harmony.
B) ✅ Good — A firm (but not squeezing) grip, three seconds of direct eye contact, and a clear "Pleasure to meet you, [Name]."
This projects "Status through Competence." It shows you are a reliable peer who is neither submissive (submissive) nor aggressive.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — A limp, soft handshake while looking at the floor to show you are humble.
This is submissive. It fails to project the "Reliability" and expertise expected of a rising professional.
Historical Context: The handshake evolved from a gesture to show one was not carrying a weapon. In the Flemish guild towns, a master craftsman's handshake sealed contracts before any paper was signed. In the modern Belgian middle class, it is a "Calibration Tool" used to measure a man's confidence and social awareness.

Question 2: Personal Grooming – Facial Hair
Scenario: You are starting a new role in a "Smart Casual" office environment. You prefer having facial hair. What is the standard?
A) ❌ Would not do that — Let it grow naturally; a "mountain man" look shows you are authentic.
This looks unpolished. It suggests that your attention to detail in your work might be as sloppy as your grooming.
B) ✅ Good — Keep it neatly trimmed with clean lines on the cheeks and neck, or stay clean-shaven.
This signals "Reliability." It shows that you put effort into your "Professional Mirror" and understand that details matter in the "Public Arena."
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Wear a "designer stubble" but don't shave your neck to save time.
The "unshaven neck" is a common error for young men. It can make "Smart Casual" look like "Just Rolled Out of Bed," damaging your brand.
Historical Context: In the 19th-century Belgian middle class — the world of the Notables who shaped the new industrial cities of Liège, Ghent, and Antwerp — facial hair was highly regulated by fashion. Today, the rule isn't "shaved vs. beard," but "managed vs. unmanaged." A young man who is "polished" still inherits that bourgeois discipline.

Question 3: Taking a Seat in a Meeting
Scenario: You are the first to arrive in a meeting room with a large table. Where and how do you sit?
A) 🟡 Slightly different — Sit at the very head of the table to show you are a future leader.
If you are the most junior person, this can look like "Elite Arrogance." It's better to leave the power seats for the facilitators.
B) ✅ Good — Sit in a middle seat, sit upright with your hands on the table, and keep your legs parallel or slightly crossed at the ankle.
This "Stance" projects "Active Interest" and "Expertise." It avoids the "spaced-out" look of someone who doesn't belong.
C) ❌ Would not do that — Slouch low in the chair with your legs spread wide (manspreading).
This is an aggressive use of space that is seen as unrefined and disrespectful to the shared "Public Arena."
Historical Context: Occupying space is a traditional marker of status. In the old Flemish stadhuis council chambers, junior members sat on the long bench while the schepenen took the carved chairs. The Belgian middle class inherited this lesson: occupy space "Efficiently" — show presence without infringing on others.

Question 4: Adaptive Linguistics – "Gentlemanly" Correction
Scenario: A female colleague makes a minor technical mistake during her presentation. You are the only one who noticed.
A) ❌ Would not do that — Shout out the correction immediately so she doesn't keep saying the wrong thing.
This is "brash" and ruins the "Social Navigation" of her presentation. It makes you look like you are trying to diminish her merit.
B) ✅ Good — Wait until a break or send a quick, private "quality check" message: "Great points! I noticed a small typo on slide 3; just wanted to flag it before the final version goes out."
This is "Peer-based Respect." You protect her "Professional Mirror" while ensuring the "Quality" of the work is maintained.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Say nothing and tell your boss later that she doesn't know what she's talking about.
This is "underhanded" and violates the middle-class social contract of teamwork and transparency.
Historical Context: The concept of the "Gentleman" in the Belgian middle class shifted from "Protector" to "Professional Peer" across the 20th century. The goal is mutual success through civility — what the old guilds called "Honest werk onder eerlijke mensen" (honest work among honest people).

Question 5: Use of Fragrance
Scenario: You are going to a high-stakes client meeting. How do you handle cologne?
A) ❌ Would not do that — Use 5–6 sprays of a famous luxury scent so they know you have "Expensive" taste.
Overpowering scents are a major taboo. It is seen as "try-hard" and inconsiderate of the shared air in the "Arena."
B) ✅ Good — One or two sprays maximum, or no scent at all. Your "Professional Brand" should be "Clean and Neutral."
"Limited accessories and scents" is the rule. You want them to remember your "Merit," not your smell.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Use a cheap body spray from the gym locker room.
These scents are often too synthetic and "juvenile." They can lower your perceived age and expertise level.
Historical Context: In the 18th century, perfume was used to hide lack of hygiene in the courts of the Austrian Netherlands. In the modern Belgian middle class, hygiene is assumed — so fragrance is a "Hidden Detail" only for those very close to you. "Doe maar gewoon" applies even to your scent.

Question 6: The "Gentleman" Door Etiquette
Scenario: You are walking toward a door with a group of male and female colleagues.
A) 🟡 Slightly different — Rush to the front, open the door, and bow for every woman to pass through.
This can feel archaic or "over-the-top" in a modern merit-based office. It can sometimes emphasize gender over professional peerage.
B) ✅ Good — If you are at the door first, hold it open for whoever is behind you (male or female), make eye contact, and follow them in.
This is "Public Arena" civility. You show awareness and respect for the group without making it a "gender performance."
C) ❌ Would not do that — Walk through and let the door swing shut behind you.
This is unpolished behavior. It shows a lack of social awareness and lowers your "Reliability."
Historical Context: Chivalry (protecting women) has evolved into Civility (respecting everyone). In the Belgian middle class, the "Host" or "First Arrived" facilitates the movement of the group — a small daily echo of the older principle that the "Notable" makes life smoother for everyone around him.

Question 7: Tone of Voice in Conflict
Scenario: A male peer is speaking to you in an aggressive, loud tone during a disagreement.
A) ❌ Would not do that — Match his volume and get even louder to show you won't be bullied.
This is a descent into "Low Status" conflict. It destroys the "Professional Mirror" for both of you.
B) ✅ Good — Lower your own volume slightly and speak at a slower pace: "I understand you're frustrated. Let's look at the 'Quality' of the data so we can find a 'Reliable' solution."
This is "Adaptive Linguistics." By staying calm, you assert "Authority" and force the other person to match your professional level.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Walk away without saying anything and block them on all platforms.
This is "Passive." In the middle class, you are expected to navigate conflict with words, not avoidance.
Historical Context: Emotional regulation is a primary marker of the educated Belgian middle class. Losing one's temper was historically seen as a loss of "Gentlemanly" status — the kind of slip that, in a small town like Mechelen or Hasselt, would still be remembered at the next family communiefeest twenty years later.

Question 8: Accessories – The Watch
Scenario: You want to buy a watch to celebrate your first promotion. What is the "Middle Class" choice for a man aged 19–30?
A) ❌ Would not do that — A massive, gold-colored watch with fake diamonds.
This is boasting. It looks "nouveau riche" and violates "Materiële Modesty."
B) ✅ Good — A classic steel-case watch with a leather or metal strap — simple, high-quality, and functional.
This is "Professional Branding." It signals that you value "Quality" and "Durability" over "Flash."
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Use your smartphone for the time; watches are for old people.
While practical, a watch is one of the few accepted "Professional Mirrors" for men. It subtly signals that you value time and tradition.
Historical Context: The wristwatch moved from the battlefield of the First World War — where Belgian officers carried them at the Yser front — to the boardroom. It remains the "silent signal" of a man who respects the "Social Contract" of punctuality, the bedrock of "Bestaanszekerheid."

Question 9: Adaptive Linguistics – Profanity
Scenario: You are "with the guys" at a work-related social event (a after-work drinks). The conversation is relaxed.
A) ❌ Would not do that — Use as much profanity as you want to show you are "one of the boys."
This is a "Social Navigation" trap. Even in relaxed settings, the "Public Arena" rules apply. Excessive swearing makes you look unrefined.
B) ✅ Good — Keep your language clean but relaxed. You can be "Informal" without being "Vulgar."
This protects your "Professional Brand." It shows you can be "approachable" without losing your "Expert" status.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Speak in perfect, stiff formal English to show you are superior.
This is "Elite-distanced." It makes you look like you don't know how to "mirror" the social energy of the group.
Historical Context: "Linguistic Temperance" (controlling your tongue) has been a Belgian middle-class virtue for centuries, separating the professional from the "unruly." The old saying — "Praat zoals het hoort, niet zoals het uitkomt" (speak as one ought to, not as it suits you) — still rules the after-work drinks.

Question 10: Handling "Physical Space" in a Hallway
Scenario: You are walking down a narrow hallway and a senior executive is walking toward you.
A) ❌ Would not do that — Stop, flatten yourself against the wall, and bow your head.
This is submissive. It suggests you don't feel you belong in the same "Arena."
B) ✅ Good — Maintain your path but turn your shoulders slightly to give them easy passage, make eye contact, and give a brief "Good morning."
This is "Social Navigation." You acknowledge their "Authority" while maintaining your own "Stance" as a confident peer.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Walk straight through them and make them move out of your way.
This is "Elite Arrogance" or "Brashness." It creates unnecessary friction in the professional environment.
Historical Context: Hallway etiquette is the "dance" of the Public Arena. In Belgian middle-class life — from the corridors of the Wetstraat to the local town hall — men are expected to be "Solid" (not pushed around) but "Civil" (not pushing others). The young man who masters this dance is already on his way to becoming a "Notable."

Level 2: The Social Arena (Men, Age 19–30)
Question 1: The "Entry" into a Group (The Barrier)
Scenario: You arrive at a local sports club or neighborhood gathering. You see three people talking in a circle. You want to be part of the group without feeling like an intruder.
A) ❌ Would not do that — Walk in and stand right in the middle, loudly greeting everyone.
This is seen as "forced." By breaking the circle too aggressively, you create a subconscious "threat." People won't like you; they will just want to get away from your energy.
B) ✅ Good — Stand at the edge, wait for a natural pause and eye contact, then nod and say: "Mind if I join the conversation?"
This is the Likeability Factor. You respect their space first, which makes them feel safe. By asking permission, you show you are a "dignified citizen" who values others, making them want to include you.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Stand silently behind them and wait to be noticed.
This is submissive. It makes you look like you lack the confidence to be a peer. You become the person people ignore, not the person they want to work with.
Historical Context: In the old Flemish town squares — the markt of Bruges, Leuven, Tongeren — the Notables knew that a man's value was measured by how he entered a space. They practiced the "Pause at the Threshold" — waiting a second to be invited in. It signals that you are someone of substance who doesn't need to shout to be seen.

Question 2: The Superpower of the "Good Morning"
Scenario: You are walking into a new housing development where you just moved. You see a neighbor washing his car. You don't know him yet.
A) 🟡 Slightly different — Look at your phone and walk past quickly so you don't bother him.
This makes you the "arrogant" new neighbor. In a Belgian community, silence is often interpreted as looking down on others. You are closing doors before they even open.
B) ✅ Good — Give a clear nod, a smile, and a firm "Good morning!" as you pass.
This is the Belgian Superpower. It is the ultimate door-opener. It signals that you are friendly but not "pushy." You are establishing yourself as a reliable, likable neighbor who is "one of us."
C) ❌ Would not do that — Stop and start asking him how much his car cost and how much he paid for his house.
This is too familiar, too fast. It's intrusive. You'll be labeled as the "neighborhood gossip" rather than a dignified peer.
Historical Context: Belgian ancestors knew that the "Greeting" was a social contract. To greet someone in the village street was to say: "I see you, I respect you, and I am not a threat." Whoever masters the greeting in a neighbourhood — whether in Antwerp, Charleroi, or Eupen — always gets the help of the carpenter or the civil servant first.

Question 3: Taking the Lead with the Bill
Scenario: You are out for a "Smart Casual" lunch with two colleagues. The bill is placed in the center of the table.
A) ✅ Good — Immediately grab the bill and say: "I've got this, don't worry about it."
This is Commanding Respect. Taking the lead (if it fits the context) shows you are a man of action and success. It makes people feel taken care of, and they will subconsciously want to return the favor later.
B) 🟡 Slightly different — Wait for the oldest person at the table to reach for it.
Safe, but you remain "invisible." You aren't opening any doors by waiting for others to lead.
C) ❌ Would not do that — Start a long debate about who had the extra coffee and calculate the cents.
This kills the "Likeability Factor." It makes you look petty. No one wants to work with the man who fights over small change; they want to work with the man who sees the "big picture."
Historical Context: The "Art of the Host" is an ancient power. In the old Flemish guilds, the person who "broke the bread" for the group held the moral authority. By managing the bill gracefully — even at a simple Brussels brasserie — you occupy that space of the "Dignified Provider."

Question 4: Body Language – The "Solid" Stance
Scenario: You are talking to a contractor about a renovation project. You want him to prioritize your house over others.
A) ❌ Would not do that — Lean against the wall, hands in pockets, looking bored.
This signals "disinterest." The contractor will think you don't care about the details, so he won't care about the quality of the work.
B) ✅ Good — Stand with your feet apart, hands visible, and look him in the eye while he speaks.
This projects Expertise. You look like a man who knows what he wants but respects the craftsman. Contractors work harder for people they respect and who show "Active Interest" in the job.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Stand very close to him and keep touching his arm to be "friendly."
This is too forced. It feels desperate. You lose your "dignity" by trying too hard to be liked.
Historical Context: Belgian craftsmen — the "Ambachtsmensen" who carry the legacy of the medieval guilds — have always looked at the "Stance." A man who stands solid (like a pillar) was seen as a man whose word — and his bank account — could be trusted. It's the posture of the "Master of the House," the man with "een steen in de maag" who has invested in real bricks and real work.

Question 5: Dealing with "The Grumpy Civil Servant"
Scenario: You need a permit, and the person behind the desk is being difficult.
A) ❌ Would not do that — Get angry and demand to see their boss.
This closes the door permanently. You become "the problem customer." In the middle-class matrix, aggression is a sign that you've lost control.
B) ✅ Good — Use "Adaptive Linguistics": "I can see you're very busy today. I really value your 'Expertise' here — what would you advise is the best way to get this 'Quality' result?"
This is the Likeability Factor at work. By complimenting their expertise and asking for "advice," you turn an enemy into a mentor. They want to help the dignified young man who respects their role.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Just keep apologizing for being there.
This is submissive. They will treat you like a child and put your file at the bottom of the pile.
Historical Context: In the Belgian town hall (or town hall), the "village notable" never fought with the clerk. He used "The Velvet Tongue" — the art of making the other person feel important so that they would do him a favor out of pride, not out of fear. This is still how permits move faster in Sint-Niklaas than in cities where men shout.

Question 6: Digital Likeability (The Group Chat)
Scenario: You are in a WhatsApp group for your local sports team or neighborhood. Someone posts a slightly annoying complaint.
A) ❌ Would not do that — Fire back an angry reply immediately to "win" the argument.
You might win the argument, but you lose the "Arena." Everyone else in the group will see you as a "troublemaker." You've just closed ten doors at once.
B) ✅ Good — Wait, then post a calm, helpful solution or a brief "Let's discuss this at the next meeting."
This is Commanding Respect. You are the "Adult in the room." You look reliable and balanced. You are the man people will call when they need a leader.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Post a bunch of laughing emojis to mock the person.
This is unpolished. It's juvenile and kills your dignity.
Historical Context: "Public Silence" was once a sign of high Belgian middle-class status. Not every dog that barks needs an answer. By staying calm in the neighbourhood-WhatsApp, you show that your "Internal Quality" is solid — the modern equivalent of the man who used to keep his peace at the parish council.

Question 7: The "Peer Mirror" (Clothing)
Scenario: You are going to a birthday party at a colleague's home. You aren't sure how formal it is.
A) 🟡 Slightly different — Wear a full suit just to be safe.
You look "arrogant" or "stiff." You make the host feel like their home isn't good enough for your high standards.
B) ✅ Good — Wear "The Middle Class Uniform": Clean chinos, a crisp shirt (no tie), and polished shoes.
This is the Likeability Factor. You look polished but approachable. You mirror the energy of a successful peer who is ready to have a good time.
C) ❌ Would not do that — Wear your old gym clothes because "it's just a house party."
This shows a lack of respect for the host. It says: "I didn't think your party was worth the effort of getting dressed."
Historical Context: The "Art of the Guest" is to honor the host. In Belgian middle-class tradition — whether arriving for a Sunday lunch in Wallonia or a verjaardag in West Flanders — by dressing well, you are paying a compliment to the house you are entering. It's the sign of a "Dignified Citizen" who respects "Architecture" — the craft of the home — even when he isn't its builder.

Question 8: The Power of Listening (The Gap)
Scenario: You are at a "Borrel" (drinks) and a senior professional is telling a long story.
A) ❌ Would not do that — Interrupt him to tell a better story about yourself.
You think you are showing success, but you are actually showing insecurity. People who boasting (boast) are rarely liked.
B) ✅ Good — Listen intently, ask one smart follow-up question, and say: "That's a fascinating insight into the 'Quality' of your industry."
This is Opening Doors. People love to talk about themselves. By being a "High-Quality Listener," you become the most likable man in the room without saying much.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Look around the room for someone "more important" while he is talking.
This is distanced. It's a social error that people never forget.
Historical Context: In the old Belgian diplomatic circles — the world of consuls, attorneys, and notaries that once ran the small post-1830 nation — the "Silent Observer" was often the most powerful person. He gathered information while everyone else was busy trying to impress him.

Question 9: Entering a Professional Space (The Building)
Scenario: You are walking into a large office building. There is a receptionist and a security guard.
A) ❌ Would not do that — Walk past them without looking, acting like you own the place.
This is "Elite Arrogance." You are making enemies of the "Gatekeepers."
B) ✅ Good — Make eye contact, give a warm "Good morning," and state your name and who you are meeting.
This is Social Intelligence. Gatekeepers (receptionists, assistants) control the flow of the building. If they like you, your path is twice as easy.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Mumble your name while looking at the floor.
This is submissive. It suggests you aren't sure if you belong there.
Historical Context: The "dignified citizen" knows that respect goes down the social ladder as well as up. In Belgian middle-class folk wisdom, a man who is kind to the waiter at the brasserie and the guard at the town hall is a man of true "Internal Quality" — the kind of man the neighbourhood eventually elects to its committees.

Question 10: Ending the Social Night
Scenario: The party or networking event is winding down. How do you leave?
A) ❌ Would not do that — Sneak out the back door so you don't have to say goodbye.
This is unpolished. You leave a "void" where your reputation should be.
B) ✅ Good — Find the host, look them in the eye, and say: "Thank you for the 'Quality' evening. It was a pleasure to be here."
This is the Lasting Impression. You end the night on a high note of "Dignity." People will remember you as the likable man who showed them respect.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Stay until the very end and help them clean up without being asked.
While helpful, it can sometimes blur the Private/Business boundary if you aren't close friends. It can look a bit "forced."
Historical Context: The "Final Handshake" is the most important one in Belgian social life — it seals the "Social Contract" of the evening. The well-greeted host remembers; the silently-departed guest is filed under "onbetrouwbaar." The next time you call, the door will be open exactly as wide as your goodbye was warm.

Level 3: Material Modesty (Men, Age 19–30)
Question 1: The "New Watch" Reveal
Scenario: You've bought a high-quality watch with your first big commission. A friend asks, "Is that a new watch? Must have cost a fortune!"
A) ❌ Would not do that — "Yeah, it's a [Brand]. It's an investment in my 'Professional Brand' — I only buy the best."
This kills the Likeability Factor. It sounds like you are reading from a textbook rather than being a dignified peer. People will think you are boasting about your status.
B) ✅ Good — "Thanks! I've always appreciated the 'Quality' and the history of this brand. It's built to last a lifetime, which I value."
This is the Secret of Quality. You justify the item through its "Durability" and "Craftsmanship." You aren't showing off your money; you are showing off your good taste and long-term thinking.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — "Oh, it's just a cheap replica, I'd never spend real money on this."
This is "False Modesty." If people find out it's real, they will think you are dishonest. A dignified citizen owns his choices without shouting about them.
Historical Context: In the past, the "Notable" in a Flemish village didn't wear gold chains to show wealth. He wore a sturdy, well-made coat that lasted twenty years. Belgian middle-class wealth has always been shown through Permanence, not flashiness — the same logic that put "een steen in de maag" of every aspiring family.

Question 2: Discussing Your "First Home"
Scenario: You've just moved out of a shared flat into a nice apartment. At a neighborhood BBQ, someone asks about your new place.
A) 🟡 Slightly different — "It's a great 'Public Arena' for me. The square footage is much higher than my last place."
Too clinical. You sound like a real estate agent, not a neighbor. It lacks the warmth that builds likeability.
B) ✅ Good — "It's a very practical space. I really value the quiet neighborhood and the 'Quality' of the light in the mornings. It helps me stay focused."
This is Dignified Citizenship. You are talking about "Quality of Life" and "Utility." It makes you relatable — everyone wants a quiet home and a good start to the day.
C) ❌ Would not do that — "It's a massive upgrade. I'm finally living where the successful people live."
This is "Elite Arrogance." It implies that your previous neighbors (or the people you are talking to) aren't successful. You're closing doors with your tongue.
Historical Context: The "Architecture" of the Belgian house was never about the size of the front door, but the warmth of the hearth. Even the proudest 19th-century "Notable" of Antwerp or Namur spoke of his home in terms of comfort, not status. To be liked, one speaks of the comfort a home provides, never the status it grants.

Question 3: The "Success" Dinner
Scenario: You've had a great month and want to treat your group of friends (who earn less than you) to dinner.
A) ❌ Would not do that — Order the most expensive champagne and say, "Drink up, guys, I'm rich today!"
This makes your friends feel submissive. You are turned from a "friend" into a "benefactor." It creates a debt that kills the joy of the evening.
B) ✅ Good — Pick a high-quality, local bistro and say, "I've had a good run lately and wanted to share the 'Quality' time with you guys. This one's on me."
This is the Likeability Factor. You acknowledge your success briefly but focus on the "Sharing" and the "Quality" of the friendship. You are a leader who brings others up with him.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Don't offer to pay at all, even though you invited them, because "everyone should pay their own merit."
This is "stiff." While technically fair, a dignified citizen knows when to be generous to open the hearts of those around him.
Historical Context: In the Flemish guilds tradition, the most successful members looked after the group. Generosity was a "Social Investment" — it ensured that if you ever fell, the group would catch you. The same logic still runs through every Belgian neighbourhood committee and verenigingsleven today.

Question 4: Handling the "Salary" Question
Scenario: A younger cousin asks you, "How much do you actually make? I want to know if my degree is worth it."
A) 🟡 Slightly different — Tell him the exact "netto" amount and show him your bank app.
This breaks the "Middle Class" taboo. It can lead to jealousy or unrealistic expectations. It lacks the "Refinement" of a professional.
B) ✅ Good — "I'm at a level where the 'Reliability' of my work is well-rewarded. It allows for a good 'Quality' of life, but the real value is the expertise I'm building."
This is Commanding Respect. You show that money is a result of merit, not the goal itself. You inspire him to focus on his "Quality" of work rather than just the paycheck.
C) ❌ Would not do that — "I don't talk about money with people like you."
This is "Elite Distance." It makes you look like a cold aristocrat. You lose the chance to be a mentor and a likable relative.
Historical Context: Belgian folk wisdom says: "Geld moet rollen, maar niet over je tong" (Money should roll, but not over your tongue). To speak of the exact sum was considered "unpolished." The middle-class man's wealth was seen in his actions — the "Bestaanszekerheid" of his family — not his audit.

⚠️ [AUTO-GENERATED] — gap-fill (Q5)
Question 5: The Car You Drive
Scenario: You can finally afford a much nicer car. Several colleagues ride to client meetings with you. Which choice fits the Belgian middle-class register best?
A) 🟡 Slightly different — Buy the loudest, most aggressive-looking sports model and rev it in the office parking lot.
This is "Dikke Nek" on wheels. It signals boasting instead of "Quality." Colleagues will start making jokes about you, not riding with you.
B) ✅ Good — Buy a well-built mid-range model in a sober colour, keep it spotlessly clean, and never mention the price.
This is "polished" applied to a vehicle. The car says "Reliability" and "Quality" without saying anything else. It is the modern echo of the Notable's well-tailored coat.
C) ❌ Would not do that — Lease the most expensive luxury brand on credit you can barely cover, just to make the right "impression."
This is "Schijn ophouden" — keeping up appearances on borrowed money. The moment a colleague learns the truth, your "professional reputation" cracks for good.
Historical Context: The Belgian middle-class rule on visible possessions has been the same since the 19th-century Notables in cities like Mechelen and Verviers: the object should look "Solid" and last twenty years. A car bought to be seen is "duur"; a car bought to last is "polished."

⚠️ [AUTO-GENERATED] — gap-fill (Q6)
Question 6: The Holiday Photo Post
Scenario: You have just returned from an expensive holiday. You want to post a picture on social media. What do you choose?
A) ❌ Would not do that — Post twenty photos of the resort, the cocktails, and the room rate written in the caption.
This is digital "boasting." Belgian peers will read it as a "Dikke Nek" performance and quietly mute you. You damage your "professional reputation" with each like-hunting post.
B) ✅ Good — Post one well-composed photo of a landscape or a small local detail, with a simple caption about the place itself.
This is "Bescheiden" digital citizenship. You share the "Quality" of the experience, not the price tag. It makes friends curious instead of resentful.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Post nothing at all and pretend the trip didn't happen.
This is "False Modesty" online. Friends who know will sense you are hiding something, which is its own small form of dishonesty.
Historical Context: The Belgian saying "Just be normal, that's already crazy enough" governs the digital "professional reputation" exactly as it once governed the village "Praat van de straat." Visibility without "Modesty" still costs the same socially as it did a century ago — only now the neighbourhood is global.

⚠️ [AUTO-GENERATED] — gap-fill (Q7)
Question 7: The Gift to a Senior Colleague
Scenario: A senior colleague is retiring after thirty years at the firm. The team is collecting for a gift. You want to add something personal.
A) 🟡 Slightly different — Give him an envelope with a large amount of cash, separately from the team gift.
Cash, alone, is "Elite Distance." It turns a personal gesture into a transaction. It can also embarrass the rest of the team whose contribution was smaller.
B) ✅ Good — Add a quality bottle of his preferred drink with a short, handwritten note thanking him for one specific lesson he taught you.
This is "polished" generosity. The note shows you actually paid attention. The gift is "Quality" without being expensive — exactly the Notable's measure.
C) ❌ Would not do that — Skip the team gift and give him something extravagant on your own to "stand out."
This is "Notable Arrogance." You break the "Rational Order" of a collective gesture to perform your own status. The team will not forget it.
Historical Context: The Belgian tradition of leaving a job — from the parish school teacher's farewell in a small Flemish town to a senior notary's quiet send-off — has always rewarded the modest, well-aimed gesture. Generosity that respects "Rational Order" is "polished." Generosity that breaks it is just boasting in a kinder costume.

⚠️ [AUTO-GENERATED] — gap-fill (Q8)
Question 8: The "Designer Label" Question
Scenario: A new colleague compliments your jacket and asks where it's from. It is from an expensive designer brand.
A) ❌ Would not do that — Show the inside label proudly and explain the brand's history and your reason for buying it.
This is boasting in slow motion. A simple compliment becomes a sales pitch about your taste and budget. You lose "Likeability" instantly.
B) ✅ Good — "Thanks — I picked it up a while ago. It's well made; I think it'll last."
This is "Bescheiden" with quiet confidence. You acknowledge the compliment, signal "Quality" through durability, and close the topic gracefully.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Mumble that it was on sale and quickly change the subject.
This is "False Modesty." The colleague usually senses the deflection. A dignified man does not need to apologise for what he chose to wear.
Historical Context: The 19th-century Belgian "Notable" in Ghent or Tournai owned perhaps two coats — both excellent — and never named the tailor. Brand-naming is a recent import; the older middle-class instinct is still that the object speaks for itself, while the man stays bescheiden.

⚠️ [AUTO-GENERATED] — gap-fill (Q9)
Question 9: The Splurge Around Family
Scenario: You bought a high-end piece of equipment (a bike, a camera, an instrument). Your less wealthy uncle asks at a family gathering, "How much did that cost?"
A) ❌ Would not do that — State the price proudly and say, "I'm at the level where I can afford the best now."
This is the cardinal "Dikke Nek." Family is precisely where boasting hurts most. You will be remembered for this single sentence at every gathering for the next decade.
B) ✅ Good — "It's a serious piece of kit. I saved up because I wanted something that would last; I plan to use it for years."
This is the "Bescheiden" answer. You honour the question without naming a number, and you reframe the spend as "Quality and Durability" rather than "showing off."
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Refuse to answer and say it's "private."
This is "Elite Distance" in a family setting. It feels colder than the room can bear. A dignified citizen finds a kinder way around the question.
Historical Context: In Belgian middle-class family culture, the "table" is sacred — the Sunday lunch, the funeral coffee gathering, the verjaardag. To bring "Money Talk" into it as a status weapon is a violation of the "Social Contract." Modesty at the table has been the rule since the 19th-century "burgerij" first sat down to eat in their own dining rooms.

Question 10: The "Hand-Me-Down" Power Move
Scenario: You are upgrading your laptop or professional tools. You want to give your old (but still high-quality) one to a younger friend.
A) 🟡 Slightly different — "I'm getting the new one because this one is too slow for my high-level work. You can have it."
This emphasizes your "Superior Merit" over theirs. It can make the gift feel like a "handout" rather than a gesture of peerage.
B) ✅ Good — "I'm switching setups, but this machine still has excellent 'Quality' and 'Reliability.' I'd love for it to go to someone who will put it to good use."
This is Opening Doors. You are passing on a tool of "Merit." You focus on the tool's quality, which preserves the dignity of the person receiving it.
C) ❌ Would not do that — Leave it in the trash and tell them where to find it so it's not "awkward."
This is "unpolished." It shows a lack of respect for the item and the person. A dignified citizen is direct and respectful in his generosity.
Historical Context: In the Belgian ambacht tradition, the "Master" would pass his tools to the "Apprentice" once they were proven. It wasn't charity; it was an investment in the "Merit" of the next generation. It's how the middle class — through every guilds, brass band, and family workshop — kept its "Expertise" strong across generations.

Level 4: High-Stakes Diplomacy (Men, Age 19–30)
Question 1: The "Respectful" Rebuttal
Scenario: A senior neighbor or older colleague is explaining a process to you, but they are clearly using outdated information that will cause a problem.
A) ❌ Would not do that — Interrupt them and say: "That's not how we do things anymore; you're living in the past."
This is "brash." You might be right, but you've just closed the door to that person's heart. They will see you as arrogant, and you lose the Likeability Factor.
B) ✅ Good — "I see the logic in that approach. Interestingly, I've seen 'Quality' results lately using [New Method]. What would you advise is the best way to bridge those two ideas?"
This is Opening Doors. You validate their experience first (Dignity), then offer the new "Quality" data as an addition rather than a correction. By asking for "advice," you keep them as an ally.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Say nothing, let them finish, and then do it your way behind their back.
This is "unreliable." In the middle-class matrix, transparency is key. If the old way fails and you knew better, your "Merit" takes a hit for not speaking up.
Historical Context: In the Belgian middle-class tradition, young men were taught the "Rule of the Two Ears." You listen twice as much as you speak. The "Dignified Citizen" doesn't win by being the loudest, but by being the one who makes the best idea feel like a group discovery — the same skill that built every successful ambachtsman's relationship with his master.

Question 2: Handling the "Undeserved" Criticism
Scenario: You are at the sports club or a community meeting, and someone blames you for a mistake you didn't make.
A) ❌ Would not do that — Get angry and start a "shouting match" to defend your honor.
This is "Low Status" behavior. The moment you lose your temper, the "Public Arena" sees you as the problem, not the other person. You lose your Commanding Respect power.
B) ✅ Good — Maintain a "Solid Stance," look them in the eye, and say calmly: "I understand there's a frustration here. Let's look at the facts together so we can ensure the 'Reliability' of the solution."
This is the Likeability Factor through strength. By staying calm while they are emotional, you automatically look like the leader. You move the conflict from "personal" to "merit-based."
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Walk away and cry so they feel bad for you.
This is submissive. It suggests you aren't ready to handle the responsibilities of a "Dignified Citizen."
Historical Context: Belgian ancestors spoke of "Geduld is een schone zaak" (Patience is a beautiful thing). The man who could stay calm under fire was often the one chosen to lead the local council, the brass band, or the parish guild. Stoic Rest and a steady gaze are the ultimate masculine "Superpowers" of the middle-class register.

Question 3: The "No" that Opens Doors
Scenario: A friend asks to borrow a significant amount of money that you aren't comfortable lending.
A) ✅ Good — "I can't lend you the cash, but I'd love to help you look at the 'Quality' of your budget or help you find a different solution. I value our friendship too much to let money get in the middle."
This is Dignified Citizenship. You set a clear boundary (Reliability) but offer a different kind of help (Merit). You protect the relationship while keeping your own "Professional Brand" as a sensible man.
B) 🟡 Slightly different — Lie and say you are completely broke.
This is unpolished. If they see you buying something nice later, the trust is broken forever.
C) ❌ Would not do that — Give them the money even though you know you'll never see it again.
This is "submissive." You are sacrificing your own "Reliability" for temporary peace. A dignified man makes decisions based on logic, not guilt.
Historical Context: Belgian folk wisdom says: "Vrienden maken is makkelijk, vrienden houden is een kunst" (Making friends is easy, keeping them is an art). The middle class learned that clear boundaries are the "fences" that keep friendships healthy for decades — the same lesson written into every well-drafted notarial act between siblings.

Question 4: Turning a Mistake into a Win
Scenario: You promised to help a neighbor with a task at 10:00 AM, but you completely forgot and show up at 2:00 PM.
A) 🟡 Slightly different — Make up a complex lie about your car breaking down.
People can usually smell a lie. It makes you look "unreliable."
B) ✅ Good — "I completely missed the mark on my timing today. I apologize for the lack of 'Reliability.' I'm here now — how can I provide the best 'Quality' help for the rest of the afternoon?"
This is Commanding Respect. By owning the mistake immediately and focusing on the "Quality" of the work now, you restore your brand. A man who apologizes without making excuses is rare and highly liked.
C) ❌ Would not do that — Don't apologize; just start working and hope they don't mention it.
This is "arrogant." It shows you don't respect the other person's time. You've just closed a door on future favors.
Historical Context: In the Belgian middle-class tradition, a man's word was his contract — the same logic that built the Antwerp diamond trade on a handshake. If the contract was broken, the only way to "fix the mirror" was through a direct, honest apology and "Over-delivery" on the next task.

Question 5: Dealing with "The Bully" in the Arena
Scenario: At a local event, someone makes a joke at your expense to make themselves look "alpha."
A) ❌ Would not do that — Insult them back with something even meaner.
You have just descended into the "Underclass" conflict style. The "Public Arena" now sees two "unpolished" men arguing.
B) ✅ Good — Give a short, dry laugh, maintain eye contact, and say: "Interesting point. Now, as we were saying about [Original Topic]..."
This is the Likeability Factor. By not being bothered, you show that their "attack" has no power over you. You look like the "Dignified Notable" who is above petty games.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Leave the event immediately so you don't have to face them.
This is "submissive." You are letting someone else control your "Arena."
Historical Context: The Notables of the past knew that the person who reacts last (or not at all) to an insult is the person with the most internal power. It's the "Secret of the Calm Sea" that every Belgian neighbourhood committee still recognises in its quiet leaders — everyone is drawn to the man who remains steady when the wind blows.

Question 6: Managing the "Hot" Debate
Scenario: The conversation at a family dinner or a group of friends turns to a very controversial political topic. Everyone is getting angry.
A) ❌ Would not do that — Pick a side and fight until everyone agrees with you.
You are destroying the "Social Arena." In the middle-class matrix, harmony is more important than being "right" in a casual setting.
B) ✅ Good — "It's clear there's a lot of 'Quality' passion here. It's a complex issue. Personally, I value [Neutral Point], but I'm more interested in how we can all enjoy this meal together."
This is Opening Doors. You act as the "Mediator." You acknowledge the passion but pivot back to the "Social Contract" of the gathering. People will be relieved and respect your maturity.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Sit in total silence and look annoyed with everyone.
This is distanced. It makes you look like you think you are better than everyone else.
Historical Context: The "Art of the Table" was a sacred thing in Flemish history. To bring "The Street" (conflict) to "The Table" (peace) was a sign of low refinement. In a country where Wetstraat politics could divide every family if allowed, the man who could keep the peace at a rowdy table was always the most liked.

⚠️ [AUTO-GENERATED] — gap-fill (Q7)
Question 7: The Linguistic Switch (NL/FR)
Scenario: You are a Flemish young man in a meeting in Brussels. A French-speaking client begins the conversation in good (but imperfect) Dutch out of politeness.
A) 🟡 Slightly different — Reply in fluent French immediately to "make it easier for him."
Although well-meant, this can read as a small correction — a quiet "your Dutch wasn't good enough." It can hurt the goodwill he just offered you.
B) ✅ Good — Continue the meeting in calm Dutch for a few minutes, thank him warmly for the effort, then offer: "Shall we switch to French if that's more comfortable for the technical part?"
This is "Adaptive Linguistics" at its highest Belgian register. You honour his gesture, then make the switch a shared decision. Both men keep their dignity; both walk out as allies.
C) ❌ Would not do that — Smirk slightly and ask, "Would you prefer if we just speak French?"
This is "Notable Arrogance." It humiliates the man who tried. You lose the deal — and the rumour will run faster than email through the Brussels professional network.
Historical Context: The Belgian language border is older than the country itself, and every municipality carries its own scars and courtesies. In middle-class business life — from Brussels to Liège to Antwerp — the well-handled language switch is one of the truest tests of "polished" professionalism. A man who passes it is trusted on both sides of the line.

⚠️ [AUTO-GENERATED] — gap-fill (Q8)
Question 8: Closing the First Big Deal
Scenario: You have just closed your first significant client deal at the office. A senior partner congratulates you in front of others.
A) ❌ Would not do that — "Yeah, I knew I'd land it — I told everyone I would."
This is "Dikke Nek" in pure form. A senior man is offering you a public moment of "professional reputation," and you spend it boasting. You will not be congratulated like that again.
B) ✅ Good — "Thank you — I learned a lot from how the team prepared the file. I'm glad the 'Quality' showed through in the end."
This is Commanding Respect through "Modesty." You accept the credit, redirect part of it to the team, and frame the win as "Quality" rather than "Conquest." Seniors remember men who behave like this.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — "Oh, it was nothing — anyone could have done it."
This is "False Modesty." Belgian middle-class culture rewards "bescheiden," not invisibility. Refusing the compliment outright tells the senior partner he was wrong to give it.
Historical Context: The 19th-century Belgian "Notable" knew that a young man's first public success was a fragile thing — easily broken by the wrong reaction. The proper answer was always to thank, to share the credit, and to keep the "professional reputation" clean for the next deal. The same logic still rules every Belgian boardroom and family business today.

⚠️ [AUTO-GENERATED] — gap-fill (Q9)
Question 9: The Loaded Family Question
Scenario: At a Sunday family lunch, an older relative asks pointedly: "And when are you going to settle down with a nice girl? You're not getting any younger."
A) ❌ Would not do that — Snap back: "That's none of your business, this is the 21st century."
This breaks the "Social Contract" of the family table. Your relative is being clumsy, not malicious. You damage the gathering for everyone present.
B) ✅ Good — Smile, give a short Belgian-style deflection: "Things are going well as they are. I'll let you know when there's news worth telling."
This is "Stoic Rest" in family form. You answer warmly, set the boundary, and close the topic without aggression. The Sunday lunch survives intact — and your "professional reputation" with it.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Look at the floor and mumble that you "haven't met the right person."
This is submissive at the family table. It invites more questions, more advice, and more well-meant intrusion. You lose your "Stance" as the man at the table.
Historical Context: The Belgian Sunday lunch — the funeral coffee gathering of slower decades, the modern brunch — has always been the test of family-sized diplomacy. The old saying "Wat aan tafel gezegd wordt, blijft aan tafel" (what is said at the table stays at the table) only works if the table itself is kept calm. Refined Grace at a family meal is the young Notable's first qualification.

Question 10: The Negotiation with the "Stubborn" Partner
Scenario: You are trying to buy a second-hand item (like a car or furniture) and the seller won't budge on a price that is too high.
A) ❌ Would not do that — "You're crazy! This isn't worth half that much."
You've just insulted their "Merit." They will now refuse to sell to you out of spite, even if you offer more later.
B) ✅ Good — "I truly respect the 'Quality' of what you have here. Based on my research, the 'Reliability' of the current market is [Price]. If you can meet me there, I can finish the deal today."
This is Commanding Respect. You compliment the item (Likeability) but stay firm on the data (Merit). You offer them a "Way Out" that lets them keep their dignity while giving you the price you want.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Just pay the full price because you hate conflict.
This is "submissive." You are losing your "Material Modesty" by overspending because you lack "Social Intelligence."
Historical Context: The Belgian "Market Square" — from the medieval Vrijdagmarkt of Ghent to the modern second-hand markets of every municipality — was where reputations were made. A man who negotiated with "The Velvet Tongue" but "The Iron Logic" was respected by everyone from the peasant to the prince. The skill survived industrialisation; it survived the euro; it still works today.

Level 5: Crisis & Reputation Management (Men, Age 19–30)
Question 1: The Public Accountability Test
Scenario: A project you were responsible for has failed, and it's being discussed in a group meeting. Your mistake was the primary cause.
A) ✅ Good — "I take full responsibility for the 'Quality' of this outcome. I've already identified the 'Reliability' gaps and have a plan to fix them."
This is Commanding Respect. A young man who admits a mistake without being submissive or making excuses is seen as having immense "Internal Strength." You turn a failure into a demonstration of leadership.
B) ❌ Would not do that — "The software was glitchy and I didn't get the support I needed from the seniors."
This is "Low Status." Shifting blame makes you look weak and "unreliable." You lose the "Professional Mirror" of a man who can handle responsibility.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Stay silent and hope no one looks at you while the boss talks.
This is "submissive." Silence in a crisis looks like guilt without the courage to own it. It kills your "Likeability Factor."
Historical Context: Belgian middle-class ancestors knew that "De vent maakt de tent" (the man makes the business). A man's reputation wasn't based on never failing, but on having the "Dignity" to stand tall and fix what he broke. From the family workshop to the modern SME, the test has not changed.

Question 2: The "Social Media" Character Attack
Scenario: Someone you had a disagreement with at the sports club posts a mocking, half-true story about you in a public group.
A) ✅ Good — Ignore the post entirely, but continue to show up at the club with a "Solid Stance" and your usual "Quality" behavior.
This is the Superpower of Silence. By not reacting, you show that the lie has no "Merit." When your real-life behavior is consistently "Dignified," the public will eventually turn against the gossip-monger, not you.
B) ❌ Would not do that — Write a long, angry reply defending yourself and exposing their secrets too.
This is a "Social Navigation" disaster. You've just invited everyone into a "dirty" Arena. You lose your dignity and look just as unpolished as the attacker.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Call them privately and beg them to take it down.
This is "submissive." It shows you are easily shaken by what others think, which lowers your "Commanding Respect" power.
Historical Context: Flemish folk wisdom says: "Hoge bomen vangen veel wind" (High trees catch a lot of wind). The more successful you become in the Belgian middle-class arena, the more people will try to blow you down. A dignified citizen stays rooted in his own "Internal Quality" — the older Belgian word for what he does when no one is watching.

Question 3: Protecting the "Social Contract" (The Ethical Whistleblow)
Scenario: You see a peer (a friend) doing something unethical that could damage the reputation of your shared group or workplace.
A) ✅ Good — "As a friend, I have to tell you: this is a breach of our 'Reliability.' I can't support this, and for the 'Quality' of our group, you need to set this right before it goes further."
This is Dignified Citizenship. You are being a "True Peer." You protect the "Arena" and the friend at the same time by holding them to a higher standard of "Merit."
B) 🟡 Slightly different — Report them secretly to the boss without talking to them first.
This is seen as "underhanded." In the middle-class matrix, a man is expected to have the courage to address a peer directly first.
C) ❌ Would not do that — Help them hide what they did because "loyalty comes first."
This is a "Low Status" move. You are now just as "unreliable" as they are. If you are caught, your "Professional Brand" is dead forever.
Historical Context: In the old Belgian guilds, members were responsible for each other's "Merit." If one man cheated, the whole guilds's reputation suffered — and a master could be expelled by his own peers. A "Gentleman" of the modern middle class still protects the standard, not the shadow.

Question 4: Handling a Crisis of "Material Modesty"
Scenario: The company is facing layoffs, but you just received a high-end luxury item (like a car or watch) that you ordered months ago.
A) ✅ Good — Keep it in the garage or "Private Arena" for now. Showing it off during a crisis lacks "Social Intelligence" and likeability.
This is Opening Doors. You show empathy for those struggling around you. It's the "Secret of the Notable": you don't rub your success in the faces of those who are hurting.
B) ❌ Would not do that — Drive it to work every day and park it right in the front to show you are "Recession-proof."
This is "Elite Arrogance." It creates a massive wall between you and your peers. You will be hated, and your "Reliability" as a team player will vanish.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Sell it immediately at a loss because you feel guilty.
This is "submissive." You shouldn't be ashamed of your merit, but you should be "bescheiden" (modest) about the timing of its display.
Historical Context: The Belgian "Notable" was the one who could be rich without being "duur" (expensive/flashy). In times of strike or recession — and Belgium's industrial cities have known many — the wealthy merchant didn't feast in the street. He practiced "Social Temperance," the same restraint that still keeps a neighbourhood together when work is scarce.

Question 5: The "Dignified" Conflict Resolution
Scenario: A male peer has insulted your expertise in front of a client to make himself look better.
A) ✅ Good — Wait until the client leaves, then say: "Your comment earlier wasn't about 'Quality'; it was about ego. I expect us to maintain a 'Professional Mirror' in the future. How do we ensure that?"
This is Commanding Respect. You address the behavior directly but professionally. You don't get "physical" or "loud," which shows you have high-level emotional control.
B) ❌ Would not do that — Punch them in the parking lot.
This is a total failure of "Social Navigation." You will be fired, and your reputation as an "unrefined" man will follow you everywhere.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Do nothing and hope the client didn't notice.
This is "submissive." If you don't set the boundary, the "Bully" will continue to use you as a stepping stone.
Historical Context: In the past, "Honor" was defended through duels — and in 19th-century Brussels, even Belgian officers sometimes met at dawn over a printed insult. In the modern Belgian middle class, the "Duel" is linguistic. The man who can master his words and his nerves wins the "Arena."

Question 6: Managing a "Public Arena" Scandal
Scenario: You are out at a bar and a friend you are with starts a fight. You are associated with him in the eyes of the onlookers.
A) ✅ Good — Step in to de-escalate without using violence, then remove your friend from the situation immediately.
This is Dignified Citizenship. You are the "Adult." You protect the "Public Arena" and your own "Reliability" by being the one who ends the chaos.
B) ❌ Would not do that — Join the fight to show your friend you have his back.
This is "Low Status" behavior. You have just destroyed your "Professional Brand" for anyone who sees you.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Run away and pretend you don't know him.
This is submissive. While it saves your skin, it shows a lack of character and "Loyalty" within the "Social Contract."
Historical Context: Belgian folk wisdom says: "Vertel me wie je vrienden zijn, en ik zal je zeggen wie je bent" (Tell me who your friends are, and I will tell you who you are). A dignified man manages his company as carefully as his finances — and in a country where every neighbourhood café has a long memory, the lesson is enforced quickly.

Question 7: The "Graceful" Career Pivot
Scenario: You have been fired or asked to leave a role. You are meeting your team for the last time.
A) ✅ Good — "I've valued the 'Quality' of our work together. I'm moving on to focus on new 'Merit' goals. I wish you all 'Reliability' and success."
This is the Lasting Impression. You leave with your head high. This makes you "Likeable" even in defeat, and ensures your network remains an open door for the future.
B) ❌ Would not do that — Tell everyone how much you hate the boss and how the company is sinking anyway.
This is "unpolished." You look bitter and "unreliable." You are burning bridges that you might need to cross ten years from now.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Cry and ask everyone to sign a petition to keep you.
This is "submissive." It lacks the "Stance" of a man who knows his own value outside of a single job title.
Historical Context: The Belgian "Notable" knows that life is a series of "Chapters." A dignified man finishes a chapter with a clean "Full Stop," never a "Messy Blot." It is the same principle that runs through every well-handled farewell — from the parish school to the boardroom — across the Belgian middle class.

⚠️ [AUTO-GENERATED] — gap-fill (Q8)
Question 8: The Whisper Network Test
Scenario: A colleague you barely know calls you discreetly to share serious accusations about a senior man at your firm. They want you to "spread the word" before any official complaint is filed.
A) ❌ Would not do that — Forward the accusations to a few trusted colleagues "so they're warned."
This is "Roddel" (gossip) dressed up as concern. You spread an unverified story and damage a man's "professional reputation" before any process. If the truth turns out differently, your own "Reliability" goes down with it.
B) ✅ Good — Listen calmly, take the matter seriously, and tell the caller: "If this is real, it deserves the proper channel — HR, the works council, or a lawyer. I won't carry this story sideways."
This is "Refined Grace" under pressure. You honour the seriousness of the claim, refuse to become a node of unverified rumour, and point toward the institutions designed to handle it. Both your "professional reputation" and the truth are protected.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Pretend you didn't hear the accusations and change the subject.
This is "distanced." If the claim turns out to be true and you stayed silent, you become part of the problem. A dignified man does not pretend to be deaf in a real crisis.
Historical Context: Belgian middle-class life has always run on parallel tracks: the official tracks of the municipality, the courts, the works council — and the unofficial tracks of neighbourhood and family rumour. The young Notable learns early that the two must not be confused. To carry a serious accusation through gossip is to wound twice: the accused, if innocent, and the truth itself, if real.

⚠️ [AUTO-GENERATED] — gap-fill (Q9)
Question 9: The Public Apology
Scenario: You have made a clearly unfair comment online about a colleague that has now spread within the firm. Several people have seen it. The colleague has not responded publicly.
A) 🟡 Slightly different — Delete the post quietly and hope it blows over.
This is "Low Status" management of your own "professional reputation." Everyone has already seen it; deletion looks like cowardice. You leave the colleague to absorb the damage you caused.
B) ✅ Good — Call the colleague first, apologise directly, and only afterwards post a short, sincere public correction without excuses or self-pity.
This is Commanding Respect even in self-inflicted damage. You honour the colleague before the audience, you own the mistake without performing it, and you give the public the only thing that closes a "professional reputation" wound: a clean, dignified apology.
C) ❌ Would not do that — Defend the original post by claiming it was "just a joke" and the colleague is "too sensitive."
This is "Dikke Nek" wrapped in self-defence. You double the original wound, you discredit the colleague again in public, and you mark yourself as a man who will never be entrusted with anything fragile again.
Historical Context: The Belgian middle-class tradition has always treated a public apology as a serious adult act — from the pulpit, from the village hall, and now from the keyboard. The 19th-century "Notable" who had spoken too sharply at a council meeting would visit the offended party in person before saying a word in public again. The order has not changed; only the speed has.

Question 10: The Leadership Crisis (The Moral Compass)
Scenario: A group of peers wants to take a "shortcut" that is legally grey but highly profitable. They want you to lead the move.
A) ✅ Good — "The 'Quality' of my leadership is based on long-term 'Reliability.' This shortcut puts our entire 'Arena' at risk. We will find a 'Merit-based' way to get these results, or we won't do it at all."
This is Commanding Respect. You show that your "Merit" is not for sale. This makes you the man people trust with the biggest, most important projects. You become a "Pillar" of the community.
B) ❌ Would not do that — Do it, but make sure your name isn't on any of the paperwork.
This is "underhanded." It shows you are a coward. If it fails, your reputation is gone; if it succeeds, you are still a man who cannot trust himself.
C) 🟡 Slightly different — Say yes because you are afraid the group will think you aren't "edgy" enough.
This is "submissive" to peer pressure. A dignified citizen follows his own compass, not the wind of the crowd.
Historical Context: Belgian folk wisdom says: "Honest duurt het langst" (Honesty lasts the longest). The middle class rose to power in the new 1830 nation because they were more reliable than the corrupt systems before them — the merchant who paid his debts, the civil servant who refused the envelope. The young man's "Honesty" is still his true wealth, and still the foundation of his "Security of livelihood."
